hashing.title: Two Sum
hashing.description: Find two numbers in an array that add up to a target using hash map for O(n) solution

steps.title: Initialize Hash Map
steps.explanation:
Create a hash map to store numbers we've seen and their indices.

Key insight: Instead of checking every pair (O(n²)), we'll check if the complement exists in O(1) time!

For target=9 and nums=[2,7,11,15], when we see 2, we look for 7.
steps.insight: Hash maps provide O(1) average lookup time, which is the key to optimizing from O(n²) to O(n)!

steps.title: Calculate Complement
steps.explanation:
For each number, calculate what value would sum with it to reach the target.

complement = target - current number

Example: target=9, current=2, complement=7
We need to find if 7 exists in our map!
steps.visualization:
i=0, nums[0]=2
target = 9
complement = 9 - 2 = 7

Question: Have we seen 7 before?
steps.insight: This transforms the problem from 'find two numbers that sum to target' to 'find if complement exists'!

steps.title: Check if Complement Exists
steps.explanation:
Look up the complement in our hash map.

If found: We've found our pair! Return both indices.
If not found: Continue to next number.

This is where the O(1) lookup saves us!
steps.visualization:
Iteration 1:
map = {}, looking for 7
7 not in map ✗

Iteration 2:
map = {2: 0}, looking for 2
2 in map ✓
Found pair: [0, 1]
steps.insight: We find the answer in a single pass! No need to check every possible pair.

steps.title: Store Current Number
steps.explanation:
If we haven't found the complement, store the current number and its index in the map.

This builds our 'seen' database for future lookups.

map.set(number, index)
steps.insight: We only need to store numbers we've seen BEFORE the current one. This prevents using the same element twice!

steps.title: Return Result or Empty
steps.explanation:
If we find a pair, return immediately with both indices.

If we finish the loop without finding a pair, return empty array.

Time: O(n) - single pass through array
Space: O(n) - hash map stores up to n elements
steps.visualization:
Example trace for [2,7,11,15], target=9:

i=0: map={}, add 2→0
i=1: map={2:0}, found 2! return [0,1]

Answer: indices [0, 1] → nums[0]+nums[1]=2+7=9 ✓
steps.insight: This is a classic space-time tradeoff: we use O(n) extra space to achieve O(n) time instead of O(n²)!

linked-lists.title: Reverse Linked List
linked-lists.description: Reverse a singly linked list iteratively by changing next pointers

code.python:
def reverseList(head):
    prev = None
    current = head
    
    while current:
        next_temp = current.next
        current.next = prev
        prev = current
        current = next_temp
    
    return prev

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.explanation:
We need three pointers:
• prev: starts as null (will become new tail)
• current: starts at head (node we're processing)
• nextTemp: temporary storage for next node

These pointers will help us reverse the links!
steps.visualization:
Original list: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → null

prev = null
current = 1

Goal: null ← 1 ← 2 ← 3 ← 4 ← 5
steps.insight: We need prev to point to the previous node, and nextTemp to avoid losing the rest of the list when we reverse the pointer!

steps.title: Save Next Node
steps.explanation:
Before changing current.next, save the next node!

If we don't do this, we'll lose access to the rest of the list.

nextTemp = current.next
steps.visualization:
current = 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5

nextTemp = 2

Now we can safely modify current.next without losing the list!
steps.insight: This is crucial! Without saving next, we'd lose the entire rest of the list when we reverse the pointer.

steps.title: Reverse the Pointer
steps.explanation:
Change current's next pointer to point backwards to prev.

This is the actual reversal step!

current.next = prev
steps.visualization:
Before: prev ← 1 → 2 → 3
After:  prev ← 1 ← 2 → 3
                ↑
              current

We've reversed one link!
steps.insight: Each iteration reverses exactly one pointer. After n iterations, all pointers are reversed!

steps.title: Move Pointers Forward
steps.explanation:
Advance both pointers one step forward:
• prev moves to current
• current moves to nextTemp

This prepares us for the next iteration!
steps.visualization:
Before move:
prev=null, current=1, nextTemp=2

After move:
prev=1, current=2

Ready to reverse next link!
steps.insight: We're essentially sliding a window of 3 pointers (prev, current, next) through the list!

steps.title: Return New Head
steps.explanation:
When current becomes null, we've processed all nodes.

prev now points to the last node of the original list, which is the new head!

Time: O(n) - visit each node once
Space: O(1) - only use 3 pointers
steps.visualization:
Final state:
current = null
prev = 5 (new head)

Reversed: null ← 1 ← 2 ← 3 ← 4 ← 5
                                  ↑
                                prev

Return prev as new head!
steps.insight: This iterative approach is more space-efficient than recursion (O(1) vs O(n) space)!

heaps.title: Kth Largest Element in Array
heaps.description: Find the kth largest element using a min heap of size k for O(n log k) solution

steps.title: Initialize Min Heap of Size K
steps.explanation:
Create a min heap that will store exactly k elements.

Key insight: If we maintain the k largest elements in a min heap, the root (minimum of these k elements) is the kth largest overall!

Example: For [3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2, heap will contain [5,6], root=5 is 2nd largest.
steps.visualization:
nums = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k = 2

Goal: Keep 2 largest elements
Heap will be: [5,6]
Root (min of heap) = 5 = 2nd largest!
steps.insight: Using a MIN heap for LARGEST elements seems counterintuitive, but it's brilliant! The min element of the k largest is exactly the kth largest!

steps.title: Build Initial Heap
steps.explanation:
Add the first k elements to the heap.

For each addition, heapify up to maintain the min heap property (parent ≤ children).

After this, we have k elements in the heap.
steps.visualization:
nums = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2

Add 3: heap = [3]
Add 2: heap = [2,3]

Min heap property maintained!
Root = 2 (smallest of first 2 elements)
steps.insight: The first k elements give us our initial set of candidates. We'll improve this set as we see more elements!

steps.title: Process Remaining Elements
steps.explanation:
For each remaining element:
• If it's larger than the heap root (smallest of k largest), it belongs in our top k!
• Replace root with new element and heapify down
• If it's smaller, ignore it (not in top k)
steps.visualization:
heap = [2,3], processing 1:
1 < 2 (root), ignore

heap = [2,3], processing 5:
5 > 2 (root), replace!
heap = [3,5]

heap = [3,5], processing 6:
6 > 3 (root), replace!
heap = [5,6]
steps.insight: We only do O(log k) work per element, not O(log n)! This is why heap approach is efficient for large n and small k.

steps.title: Heapify Down After Replacement
steps.explanation:
When we replace the root, we might violate the min heap property.

Heapify down: Compare with children and swap with smaller child until heap property is restored.

This maintains our invariant: heap contains k largest elements.
steps.visualization:
After replacing root 3 with 6:

Before heapify:  After heapify:
      6              5
     / \            / \n    5   ?          6   ?

Heap property restored!
steps.insight: Heapify down is O(log k) because the heap height is log k. This is much better than sorting the entire array!

steps.title: Return Heap Root
steps.explanation:
After processing all elements, the heap contains the k largest elements.

The root (minimum of these k elements) is the kth largest element!

Time: O(n log k) - process n elements, each takes O(log k)
Space: O(k) - heap stores k elements
steps.visualization:
Final heap for [3,2,1,5,6,4], k=2:

heap = [5,6]
        ↑
      root

Return 5 (2nd largest element)

All elements: [1,2,3,4,5,6]
2nd largest: 5 ✓
steps.insight: This approach is optimal when k << n. For k=1, it's O(n). For k=n, sorting might be better. Choose based on k!

graph-algorithms.title: Course Schedule (Cycle Detection)
graph-algorithms.description: Determine if you can finish all courses given prerequisites using topological sort and cycle detection

steps.title: Build Dependency Graph
steps.explanation:
Create an adjacency list where graph[A] contains all courses that depend on course A.

Example: [0,1] means course 0 depends on course 1
So graph[1] = [0] (taking course 1 unlocks course 0)

This represents the prerequisite relationships!
steps.visualization:
prerequisites = [[1,0], [2,1], [0,2]]

graph:
0 → [2]
1 → [0]
2 → [1]

This forms a cycle: 0→2→1→0
steps.insight: Building the graph in this direction (prereq → course) makes it easy to detect cycles using DFS!

steps.title: Initialize Visited States
steps.explanation:
Use 3 states to track each course:
• 0 = unvisited (haven't checked yet)
• 1 = visiting (currently in DFS path)
• 2 = visited (fully processed, no cycle)

State 1 is key for cycle detection!
steps.visualization:
visited = [0, 0, 0, 0]
           ↑  ↑  ↑  ↑
           course 0,1,2,3

All start as unvisited
steps.insight: The 'visiting' state (1) lets us detect back edges, which indicate cycles!

steps.title: Detect Cycle with DFS
steps.explanation:
For each course, do DFS:
• If we encounter a course marked 'visiting' (1), we've found a cycle!
• If already 'visited' (2), skip it (already checked)
• Otherwise, mark as 'visiting' and explore neighbors
steps.visualization:
DFS from course 0:
visited[0] = 1 (visiting)
Explore 0→2
visited[2] = 1 (visiting)
Explore 2→1
visited[1] = 1 (visiting)
Explore 1→0
visited[0] = 1 (visiting) ← CYCLE!
steps.insight: When we see a 'visiting' node again, it means we've returned to a node in our current path - that's a cycle!

steps.title: Mark as Fully Visited
steps.explanation:
After exploring all neighbors without finding a cycle, mark the course as 'visited' (2).

This means: 'I've checked this course and all its dependencies - no cycles here!'

This optimization prevents re-checking the same paths.
steps.visualization:
After DFS from course 3 (no cycle):

visited[3] = 2 (fully visited)

If we encounter course 3 again in another path, we can skip it!
steps.insight: Marking as 'visited' (2) is an optimization. Without it, we'd still detect cycles, but we'd waste time re-exploring safe paths!

steps.title: Check All Courses
steps.explanation:
Try DFS from each unvisited course.

If any DFS finds a cycle, return false (impossible to finish all courses).

If all DFS complete without cycles, return true!

Time: O(V+E) - visit each vertex and edge once
Space: O(V) - recursion stack and visited array
steps.visualization:
Example 1: [[1,0]] (0→1)
No cycle! Return true

Example 2: [[1,0],[0,1]] (0→1→0)
Cycle detected! Return false

A cycle means circular dependencies - impossible to complete!
steps.insight: This is topological sort with cycle detection! If a valid topological order exists, there's no cycle and we can finish all courses.
